title: How does the new Date-Time API (java.time) differ from the previous java.util.Date and java.util.Calendar classes?

answers.junior: The java.time API, introduced in Java 8 and standardized as JSR-310, fundamentally redesigns date-time handling to address critical flaws in java.util.Date and java.util.Calendar. The most significant improvement is immutability—all java.time classes are immutable and thread-safe by design, whereas Date and Calendar are mutable, making them prone to race conditions and requiring defensive copying in concurrent environments.
answers.middle: Rather than a single monolithic Date class, java.time provides purpose-specific classes: LocalDate for dates without time, LocalTime for times without dates, LocalDateTime for both, and ZonedDateTime for timezone-aware operations, eliminating the confusing multi-role design where Date combined date, time, and milliseconds since epoch. Month and DayOfWeek are proper enums instead of integer constants (Calendar.JANUARY = 0), providing type safety and preventing subtle bugs like off-by-one errors. The API strictly follows ISO-8601 standards, with months 1-12 and days 1-31, while Calendar uses zero-indexed months (January = 0), a notorious source of confusion.
answers.senior: Timezone handling is dramatically improved through ZoneId and ZoneRules, supporting daylight saving time transitions correctly, whereas Calendar's timezone implementation was error-prone and often required workarounds. The fluent, method-chaining design enables readable code like LocalDate.now().plusDays(10).withMonth(3), contrasting sharply with Calendar's clunky add() and set() methods that return the same instance, making code less expressive. Formatting and parsing are delegated to DateTimeFormatter with proper immutability and thread-safety, whereas SimpleDateFormat is notoriously not thread-safe and caused countless production bugs.
answers.tricky: The API also handles edge cases better—distinguishing between local time (no timezone context) and zoned time prevents the errors that occurred when Calendar silently applied system timezone assumptions. For legacy compatibility, java.time provides conversion methods with java.util.Date through Instant and temporal conversion utilities, allowing gradual migration of existing codebases.
